text: from the Division of Mentally Invasive Surgery, in the Department of Surgery at Houston Methodist Hospital, and MITEI, the Methodist Institute for Technology, Innovation, and Education.

Our patient was a female in the sixth decade of life with a known diagnosis of gallstones. She presented to an outside facility with right upper quadrant and right-sided pain which radiated both through to the back and to the right subscapular region. The patient underwent an abdominal ultrasound, an abdominal CT scan, and an abdominal MRI at the outside facility. Her outside physicians, based upon a review of her studies, referred her to our practice with the diagnosis of a type I choledochal cyst.
The patient specifically requested a laparoscopic approach to her surgical care.

Prior to her visit with us, she was referred by us to a senior gastroenterologist at our facility for an ERCP to better define her ductal anatomy. An endoscopic ultrasound was performed at the same time.

Her endoscopic ultrasound showed shadowing gallstones filling the common bile duct all the way down to the ampulla. Her ERCP showed that she did not have a type I choledochal cyst. The fusiform shape of her common bile duct was due to its being filled with gallstones that tapered into the ampulla. The cystic duct and gallbladder were not visualized.
A biliary stent is visibly placed within the common bile duct to facilitate drainage and relieve obstruction caused by gallstones.
She was consented and scheduled for a laparoscopic cholecystectomy with common bile duct exploration and removal of stones.
The surgeon observes a markedly contracted and distorted gallbladder, with Hartmann's pouch tightly packed with gallstones.
Dense adhesions are visible inferiorly and medially, obscuring the common bile duct. The surgeon decides to open the gallbladder just lateral to Hartman's pouch.
The surgeon opens the gallbladder widely, exposing numerous large gallstones. Each gallstone is carefully removed and placed into a plastic specimen retrieval bag.
The surgeon opens Hartman's pouch inferiorly, extracting additional gallstones and placing them into a plastic specimen retrieval bag. The gallbladder is then irrigated to remove debris.
The posterior wall of the gallbladder reveals a mucosal line opening greater than five millimeters in diameter, identified as a major bile duct from Segment V draining into the gallbladder bed.
A three-milliliter choledochoscope is inserted into the opening, confirming the presence of a major hepatic duct draining directly into the gallbladder, consistent with a cholecystohepatic duct.

The choledochoscope is withdrawn and blindly directed distally into Hartman's pouch. The previously placed stent is visible at the ampulla of Vater. The scope is advanced through the ampulla, pushing stone fragments and debris into the duodenum, before being withdrawn.

Direct visualization into Hartman's pouch shows a fistula comprising more than fifty percent of the common bile duct diameter, indicative of Moritzi syndrome, type IIb. The surgeon identifies a combination of a cholecystohepatic duct and Moritzi syndrome, type IIb.
The surgeon prepares to perform a Roux-en-Y cholecystogeogenostomy to drain both the cholecystohepatic duct and the Moritzi syndrome fistula through the remnant of a fenestrated gallbladder.
The surgeon excises and removes the distal portion of the gallbladder, completing the resection of this anatomical structure.

The surgeon dissects lower midline adhesions to gain access for creating a Roux limb. A standard Roux-en-Y jejunostomy is performed, involving a 40 cm biliopancreatic limb, a 60 cm Roux limb, and a 2.5 cm stapled side-to-side jejuno-jejunostomy.
The surgeon uses sutures to close the common enterotomy, ensuring the incision in the intestinal wall is securely sealed.
The surgeon creates a window through the transverse mesocolon just anterior to the duodenum, allowing the Roux limb to be passed cranially into the operative field.
The surgeon places a posterior row of running III-O polydioxynone suture, starting the suture line inferiorly and tying it before running the suture from caudal to cranial.
Upon reaching the apex of the suture line, the surgeon opens the bowel for anastomosis using a 3mm hook cautery, visibly creating an incision in the bowel tissue.
The surgeon places a second, anterior row of running 3-0 polydioxanone suture, starting inferiorly, tying it, and then running the suture from caudal to cranial along the anastomosis.
At the apex of the suture line, the surgeon ties the two suture lines together, completing the anastomosis.
The surgeon secures the roux limb to the gallbladder bed using a 3-0 polyglactin suture, ensuring stability and preventing rotation or tension on the anastomosis when the patient is upright.
The surgeon places a 15 French fluted silastic drain through the operative field, marking the conclusion of the operation.
She was begun on a clear liquid diet on post-op day one. She was begun on a full liquid diet on post-op day two. Her drain was removed and she was discharged to home on post-op day three.

An ERCP done at the time of her post-operative removal of her biliary stent shows a patent anastomosis and good filling of the biliary tree. The roux limb could not be filled with enough pressure to delineate the cholecystohepatic duct.

Cholecystohepatic duct is a very rare bile duct anomaly with a frequency of zero point seven percent to one point two percent. Moritzi syndrome is an equally uncommon entity in developed countries with a frequency of one percent to two percent. It is more common in countries with reduced access to medical care occurring with a frequency of four point seven percent to five point seven percent. We believe that this is the first reported case of this extremely uncommon combination of very uncommon diseases.
